chunk_summary: Diagnostic approaches for food allergy in infants under six months focus on avoiding overdiagnosis, using targeted elimination diets, and age-appropriate testing strategies to differentiate between IgE and non-IgE mediated conditions.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic challenges of food allergies in infants under six months.
Learn appropriate elimination diet strategies for suspected cow’s milk protein allergy (CMPA).
Differentiate between IgE-mediated and non-IgE-mediated food allergy presentations in infants.
Recognize red flags that warrant further investigation beyond food allergy.
key_facts: Infants under six months are prone to overdiagnosis due to symptom overlap with colic, reflux, and loose stools.
For suspected immune-mediated CMPA, use single-food cow’s milk protein elimination with a short trial period and objective endpoints.
In exclusively breastfed infants, maternal dairy elimination is recommended, with calcium and vitamin D supplementation if prolonged.
For formula-fed infants, start with extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) and escalate to amino acid formula (AAF) only for severe cases or eHF failure.
Avoid soy formula in infants under six months due to higher cross-reactivity and weaker evidence.
Red flags like poor growth, persistent vomiting, dehydration, or large-volume blood require evaluation for non-allergic causes.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Infants under 6 months are vulnerable to overdiagnosis because common conditions (colic, reflux, loose stools) overlap with non-IgE CMPA phenotypes. Use higher diagnostic thresholds and narrower trials. (Source: Cow’s Milk Protein Allergy, IAP Chapter 075)
document_key_topics: Diagnostic strategies for food allergies in infants
Management of cow’s milk protein allergy (CMPA)
Red flags for non-allergic conditions in infants
